title: Login redirect loop after SSO change
body:
We recently switched our company's SSO provider from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID. Since the switch, some users are getting stuck in an infinite redirect loop when trying to log into TaskFlow. They click login, get redirected to Microsoft, authenticate successfully, get redirected back to TaskFlow, and then immediately get sent back to Microsoft again. Not all users are affected — maybe 30% of our team. The rest can log in fine.

We've already verified that the Entra ID app registration is correct and matches our redirect URIs. The affected users can log into other apps that use the same SSO just fine.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: Thanks for the detailed report — that redirect loop sounds frustrating. One thing that would really help narrow this down: have you noticed any pattern among the 30% of affected users? For example, do they share anything in common like specific roles or groups in Entra ID, a particular browser or OS, or maybe they had existing TaskFlow sessions open during the SSO migration? Anything that distinguishes them from the users who can log in fine would be really valuable.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question. I've been trying to figure out the pattern myself. One thing I noticed is that a lot of the affected users have those plus-sign email addresses — like jane+taskflow@company.com. We use plus addressing for some people to help with email filtering. I'm not 100% sure that's the only thing though, because I think there are a couple users affected who don't have the plus sign but I know they had their email changed or aliased at some point when we set up Entra. The users who work fine seem to just have straightforward email addresses that are the same as what they had in Okta. Oh also, I noticed that some affected users said they could sometimes get in using an incognito window, which is weird.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really helpful observation about the plus-sign addresses and email changes — and the incognito detail is a great clue too.

Could you (or someone from your team) check TaskFlow's authentication logs for one of the affected users during a failed login attempt? Specifically, I'm trying to understand what happens when TaskFlow receives the callback from Entra ID — does it log the email claim it receives, and does it show any error like "user not found" or a session/token mismatch before the redirect kicks in? If you don't have easy access to auth logs, even a browser Network tab capture (from the redirect loop) showing the callback URL and any error parameters would help a lot.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't have great access to TaskFlow's internal auth logs — we're self-hosted but I'm more of an IT admin than a developer, so I'm not sure where those live exactly. I did have one of the affected users open the browser Network tab though while it was looping. From what I could see, the callback from Entra seemed to come back fine — like it had a successful authentication. I noticed there was a Set-Cookie header in one of the redirect responses, but then it just kept redirecting back to Microsoft again. I didn't see an obvious error message in the URL or anything like 'user not found' — it just seemed like TaskFlow wasn't recognizing that they'd logged in. I'm not sure if the cookie was actually getting saved or not, but the incognito thing working sometimes makes me think it's something cookie-related maybe? I can try to get a HAR file export or screenshots of the network tab if that would help.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for getting that network capture — the cookie detail is really useful. I'd like to try a quick test to narrow down whether this is a cookie issue, an email matching issue, or both. Could you pick one of the affected users (ideally one with a plus-sign address) and have them do the following:

1. **Clear all TaskFlow cookies specifically** (not just use incognito — actually go into browser settings and delete any cookies for your TaskFlow domain), then try logging in again.
2. Let me know: does it work after clearing cookies, or does the loop still happen?

This will tell us a lot. If clearing cookies fixes it, the old Okta session data is likely interfering with the new Entra flow. If the loop persists even after clearing cookies, it points more toward an email mismatch between what Entra sends and what TaskFlow has stored for that user. Either way, it gives us a concrete reproduction path.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I actually already tried something like that with a couple users last week. One of them (she has a plus address) cleared all her TaskFlow cookies and tried again — it worked the first time, but then when she closed her browser and came back later, the loop started again. Another user tried the same thing and it didn't help at all, though I think he was one of the ones whose email got aliased during the Entra setup rather than a plus address user. So it seems like clearing cookies helps sometimes but it's not a reliable fix. It kind of lines up with the incognito thing — like sometimes it works on a fresh start but not consistently. Does that help narrow it down?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful — the inconsistent results from clearing cookies actually tell us a lot. I think we can pin this down with one more check. Since you're a self-hosted admin, you likely have access to your database or user management panel. Could you compare the following for one of the affected plus-sign users:

1. **What email does TaskFlow have stored for them?** (Check the user profile in TaskFlow's admin panel or user table.)
2. **What email does Entra ID send as the login claim?** (In your Entra ID admin portal, check the user's primary email or UPN — the value that gets sent to apps during sign-in.)

If those two values don't match exactly — for example, TaskFlow has `jane+taskflow@company.com` but Entra sends `jane@company.com`, or vice versa — that would explain everything: TaskFlow gets a valid authentication but can't find a matching user, so it never creates a session and starts the loop over. Even a subtle difference (like casing or a missing alias) would cause this.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Oh that's a good idea, I hadn't thought to compare those directly. Let me check... OK so I looked at one of our plus-address users in TaskFlow's admin panel and her email is listed as jane+taskflow@company.com. Then I went into the Entra ID portal and her primary email (UPN) is just jane@company.com — Entra doesn't seem to keep the plus part. So yeah, those don't match. I'm guessing that's probably the issue, or at least part of it? For the other user who got aliased, I think something similar is going on — his email in Entra is different from what TaskFlow originally had stored for him from the Okta days. But I'm still a little confused about why the cookie clearing thing is inconsistent if it's just an email mismatch issue. Like, wouldn't it just fail every time?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Login redirect loop caused by email mismatch between Entra ID UPN and TaskFlow stored email after SSO migration","problem":"After migrating SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID, approximately 30% of users experience an infinite redirect loop when logging in. Entra ID authenticates them successfully, but TaskFlow fails to establish a session and redirects them back to the IdP. Affected users disproportionately have plus-sign email addresses (e.g., jane+taskflow@company.com) or had their email aliased during the Entra setup.","root_cause_hypothesis":"Two overlapping issues: (1) **Primary — email mismatch:** TaskFlow performs user lookup by exact email match against the identity claim from the IdP callback. Entra ID sends the UPN (jane@company.com) which differs from the plus-addressed or aliased email stored in TaskFlow (jane+taskflow@company.com). The lookup fails, no session is created, and TaskFlow's auth middleware redirects back to the IdP, creating the loop. (2) **Secondary — stale Okta session cookies:** Some users still have cookies from the Okta-era auth flow that conflict with the new Entra flow, causing session establishment to fail even when the email would otherwise match. This explains why clearing cookies helps some users temporarily (resolves the cookie conflict alone) but not others (email mismatch persists regardless of cookie state), and why incognito windows sometimes work (no old cookies, and possibly a cache/timing factor in email resolution).","reproduction_steps":["Have a user whose TaskFlow profile email includes a plus-sign (e.g., jane+taskflow@company.com) while their Entra ID UPN is the base address (jane@company.com)","User navigates to TaskFlow login page and clicks the SSO login button","User is redirected to Microsoft Entra ID and authenticates successfully","Entra ID redirects back to TaskFlow's callback URL with a valid token containing the UPN as the email claim","TaskFlow attempts to look up a user matching the email from the token, finds no match (plus-sign address vs. plain address), fails to create a session","TaskFlow's auth middleware sees no valid session and redirects back to the IdP, creating the loop"],"environment":"Self-hosted TaskFlow instance. Recently migrated SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID. Entra ID app registration and redirect URIs have been verified as correct. Affected users can log into other Entra-backed apps without issues.","severity":"high","impact":"Approximately 30% of the team cannot reliably log into TaskFlow. No dependable workaround exists — clearing cookies and incognito mode work intermittently. Affected users are locked out of the task management application.","recommended_fix":"1. **Immediate fix:** Write a migration script to normalize emails in TaskFlow's user table to match Entra ID UPNs. For plus-addressed users, update stored emails to match their Entra UPN (or add the UPN as a recognized alias). 2. **Auth code fix:** In TaskFlow's SSO callback handler, find the user lookup logic (likely an exact-match query on email). Either normalize incoming email claims (strip plus-addressing) before lookup, or implement case-insensitive lookup with alias support. 3. **Cookie fix:** Invalidate all pre-migration session cookies — either by rotating the session signing key or by clearing the sessions table/store. This resolves the stale Okta cookie interference. 4. **Preventive:** Add logging to the auth callback path that records the claimed email and whether a user match was found, so future mismatches surface in logs rather than as silent redirect loops.","proposed_test_case":"Create a test user in TaskFlow with email user+tag@example.com. Configure the test IdP to return user@example.com as the email claim. Attempt SSO login and verify that: (a) the user is matched correctly despite the plus-address difference, (b) a valid session is created, and (c) no redirect loop occurs. Additionally, test with a pre-existing session cookie from a different IdP configuration and verify it is handled gracefully (invalidated, not looped).","information_gaps":["Exact field name and matching logic in TaskFlow's auth callback code (exact match vs. case-insensitive, which OIDC claim is used — email vs. preferred_username vs. UPN)","Whether TaskFlow supports email aliases or secondary email fields that could be leveraged instead of migrating primary emails","The precise count and full list of affected users to confirm whether ALL affected users have an email discrepancy (reporter noted a couple cases were uncertain)"]}
